ontology.foodon_gap_note: No species-level FoodOn entry for Turbo/Trochus top shell; closest_parent gastropod food product used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJN covers gastropods broadly; no specific top-shell entry exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for top shell / turban shell gastropod.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of top shell in Singapore or Malaysia is approximately 200 g, comprising several whole cooked shells with sauce, served as a shared seafood dish.

nutrient_source.title: Turbo (top shell) cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Tables for Southeast Asia; adapted from mollusc gastropod cooked values
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central) and FAO/INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct USDA or HPB entry for 'top shell' (Turbo/Trochus spp.) as a prepared dish. Values adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked mollusc / gastropod composites (NDB 15167 — mollusks, whelk, cooked, moist heat) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA shellfish data. Sauce and seasoning contribution estimated at 15% of total dish weight; sodium adjusted upward accordingly. Confidence is medium due to species-level and preparation-level adaptation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of shellfish and high-protein low-carbohydrate seafood — analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (shellfish / seafood category)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Top shell (Turbo/Trochus spp.) is a high-protein, very low-carbohydrate shellfish (carbohydrate_g ~3.8 per 100 g). No direct GI measurement exists for top shell in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 5 by analogy with other cooked shellfish and molluscs (oysters GI ~5, mussels GI ~5) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (5 × 3.8 × 2) / 100 = 0.38, rounded to 0.4. gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from sauces and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g restaurant serving of top shell prepared with soy sauce and oyster sauce contributes approximately 840 mg sodium, representing 37–42% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sauce preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Top shell is a high-protein, very low-carbohydrate shellfish with an estimated GI of 5 and a glycemic load of approximately 0.4 per serving, making it a suitable protein source for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when prepared with minimal added sugars or sweet sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium from sauces and moderate cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Top shell provides lean protein and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids but the restaurant preparation with soy and oyster sauces significantly elevates sodium intake; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose steamed or lightly seasoned preparations and be mindful of the approximately 85 mg cholesterol per 100 g.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Top shell (also called turban shell or siput tudung in Malay) refers to cooked gastropod molluscs of the Turbo or Trochus species family, commonly served in Malaysian and Singaporean seafood restaurants. The dish typically consists of whole shells that have been boiled or stir-fried with aromatics such as garlic, ginger, chilli, and seasoned with soy sauce and oyster sauce. The flesh is extracted from the shell using a toothpick or small fork and has a firm, slightly chewy texture with a mild, briny oceanic flavour. In Singapore, top shell is a popular zi char (Chinese-style cooked food) and seafood restaurant item, often ordered as part of a shared meal. Nutritionally, top shell is a lean, high-protein shellfish providing approximately 18.5 g protein per 100 g with only 1.8 g fat, making it a nutrient-dense seafood choice. It is a notable source of vitamin B12 (approximately 4.5 mcg per 100 g), iron (4.2 mg), selenium, and phosphorus. The primary nutritional concern in restaurant preparations is the elevated sodium content contributed by soy sauce and oyster sauce, which can reach 840 mg per typical 200 g serving. The glycemic index is very low (estimated GI ~5), making it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose. Top shell is not halal-certified in most contexts due to Islamic dietary rulings on certain shellfish, and it contains mollusc allergens. The dish is dairy-free and gluten content depends on the sauce used.

confidence_reason: No direct species-level nutrient entry for Turbo/Trochus top shell exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Values adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked mollusc/gastropod composites (whelk, cooked) and FAO/INFOODS SEA shellfish data. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database shellfish category. Confidence is medium due to species-level and preparation-level adaptation.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked gastropod/mollusc composites (NDB 15167 whelk, cooked, moist heat) and FAO/INFOODS SEA shellfish data; no direct top-shell species entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of the access date. 2. Sodium value (420 mg per 100 g) reflects estimated contribution from soy sauce and oyster sauce seasoning typical of restaurant preparation; plain boiled top shell would have lower sodium (~200 mg per 100 g). 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted USDA gastropod source composite. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted USDA gastropod source composite. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for shellfish; molluscs do not contain industrially produced trans fats. 6. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero; shellfish contain no dietary fibre. 7. GI estimated at 5 by analogy with cooked shellfish (oysters, mussels) from University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for top shell exists. 8. Halal status is marked false as top shell (gastropod mollusc) is considered non-halal under most mainstream Islamic jurisprudence (Shafi'i school prevalent in Singapore/Malaysia); consumers should verify with their own religious authority. 9. The input food_name 'TOP SHEL' is a truncated menu label; canonical English name resolved to 'Top Shell (Shellfish)'.
